text: Senoz: So......
Senoz: Isn't writing charts the honest way pretty boring?

Senoz: You come up with an absolutely godlike pattern in your head, but when you open the chart editor you still have to create tracks one by one and adjust their positions, and by then your inspiration is gone, right?
Senoz: The Draft feature was created exactly to solve this problem. You can place free Notes that aren't on any track, and arrange them back onto tracks later.
Senoz: Let's take a look at how to use it.
Brief Introduction
Hands-On Tutorial
Press [b]Ctrl + D[/b] to toggle Draft Mode. In Draft Mode, [b]Right Click[/b] to place a draft Note.
Draft Notes support copy-paste, dragging, box selection, multi-selection, and other operations, basically the same as regular Notes and track nodes.

[b]Z Key[/b] and [b]X Key[/b] are used to change the draft Note's width, and [b]Shift+Z[/b] and [b]Shift+X[/b] snap the width to the grid. When no draft Note is selected, the preview Note's width will be modified instead.
In Draft Mode you can select tracks. Selected draft Notes and tracks can coexist at this time.

Press [b]B Key[/b] to automatically snap selected draft Notes to the closest track by position and width, turning them into live Notes. If tracks are also selected, it will only look for targets among the selected tracks.
When a track and some draft Notes are selected, press [b]H Key[/b] to convert the draft Notes into nodes of that track, used to anchor the track's movement path.
Press [b]T Key[/b] to convert each selected draft Note into an independent single track, suitable for making blink effects.
Now we have loaded the example level.
Press [b]Ctrl + D[/b] to toggle Draft Mode, then right-click to place your first draft Note.

Draft Note operations are basically the same as regular Notes and track nodes. For example, you can copy-paste, drag, and after selecting two draft Notes press [b]H Key[/b] to connect them into a Hold.

[b]Z Key[/b] and [b]X Key[/b] are used to change the draft Note's width, and [b]Shift+Z[/b] and [b]Shift+X[/b] snap the width to the grid. If no draft Note is selected, the preview Note's width will be modified instead.
Senoz: However, Ctrl+Z is undo and Ctrl+X is cut. Truly strange, isn't it? (
Anyway, let's get more drafts going——
Now try changing the draft Note's width 5 times. It's best to try it with grid on and grid off, right?
The selection mode in Draft Mode is somewhat different from normal mode.
In normal mode you cannot select draft Notes, and in Draft Mode you also cannot select regular Notes.
In normal mode Notes and tracks cannot be selected at the same time, but draft Notes can coexist with selected tracks.
Well then, the three functions introduced below are all aimed at 'promoting' draft Notes to live status.
First function: Press [b]B Key[/b] to automatically snap selected draft Notes to the closest track by position and width, turning them into live Notes.
There are two cases: If only draft Notes are selected, it will search the entire chart for the most suitable track; if some tracks are also selected, it will only look among the selected tracks.
(NEEDS UPDATE) There are 5 more tracks in the level now. Try snapping 5 draft Notes onto tracks.
Second function: When a track and some draft Notes are selected, press [b]H Key[/b] to turn the draft Notes into nodes of the track.
This means drafts can be used not only to anchor patterns, but also to anchor the approximate movement path of tracks.
Now try turning 5 draft Notes into track nodes.
Third function: Press [b]T Key[/b] to turn each selected draft Note into an independent single track. This function is useful when making blink effects.
Now try turning 5 draft Notes into individual tracks.
By the way, in the chart formatting function at the bottom you can delete all drafts at once.
That's all for the draft Note features. Hope it helps with your charting.

I'm not just a loser, I'm also a joker, I'm also a salted fish. I've never been in a relationship my whole life, timid and insecure. My youth is one fantasy after another, every day fantasizing that I could have a girl to walk around the streets with, go shopping with, hang out with. In reality I just drained my parents' money, went to a trash school, found a job after muddling through, a three-no person with no ideals, no goals, no abilities. Every time I see you making loser jokes online, I want to cry. I'm a rat underground, peeking through the sewer grate at all the beauty above ground. Every time I see this stuff it's another wound to my heart, another compression of my living space. Please bro give us clowns some room to live, I really don't want to cry my pillowcase completely wet during the day
I'm crying...
What is this...
This isn't me!
Mysterious CGs won't exist, just as there won't be a visual novel in a rhythm game chart editor.
???: Then let me introduce myself.
Senoz: I'm Senoz, an anomalous lifeform born from the convergence and fusion of humanity's intense and pure emotions.
Senoz: What was that emotion again...... "I really want TAKANA³ to have a mascot character"
Senoz: Anyway! Seeing me means you've entered the tutorial. Want some introductory lessons?
Sure!
I prefer to read the documentation directly
I'm already good at charting
Great god /bow/bow/bow /continue tag/continue tag/continue tag
There are way too few people writing TAKUMI³ charts (tear) Thank you for your support!
Senoz: Next please try to follow the instructions! Clicking randomly might cause the dialog to get stuck, and you'll have to restart the editor.
Senoz: So let's use this song as a demonstration——Stream / kyaai
Senoz: Speaking of charting after deciding what song to write what's the first thing to do?
Measure the song's BPM and offset
Senoz: Correct!
OVERCHARTING!!!
Senoz: Before overcharting you need to sync the notes to the beat first.
Not writing charts
Senoz: Roger that.
Senoz: Now I've already filled in the offset and BPM for this song for you.
Senoz: One thing to remember: all time values in this chart editor are integers in [b]milliseconds[/b].
Senoz: See the grid lines on the screen? When grid is enabled all time-related operations will snap to the grid making it easier to sync to the beat.
Senoz: At the same time, when grid is enabled you can also use the [b]mouse wheel[/b] to quickly scroll through music time by grid lines.
Senoz: Alright! So, to write a TAKUMI³ chart, first you definitely need to create a track.
Senoz: You can use [b]Shift + Right Click[/b] to create a track, and [b]Left Click[/b] to select it.
Senoz: After selecting a track, you can see a semi-transparent Note preview. At this point [b]Right Click[/b] to create a Note at the corresponding position.
Senoz: Give it a try! Create a track, then place 3 Taps on it.
Senoz: Just placing Taps isn't interesting enough right? You can use [b]` Tilde key[/b] to switch Note types, or select directly from the UI at the bottom.
Senoz: Additionally, a quick way to adjust Hold length is to select two Notes and press [b]H key[/b]. This will turn the two Notes into a Hold with a length equal to their time difference.
Senoz: Use [b]Q Key[/b] or [b]Space Key[/b] to toggle music between play and pause.
Senoz: [b]Left Click[/b] a Note to select it. Hold [b]Ctrl[/b] to multi-select, or [b]Left Click and drag[/b] to box-select multiple Notes.
Senoz: Give it a try! Place 3 Slides, then connect two Notes into a Hold.
Senoz: Next is making the tracks move.

Senoz: You've probably noticed the left and right lines when selecting a track. In TAKANA³, track movement paths are defined by [b]nodes[/b]. Each node marks the track's position at that moment, and how it will move to the next node, which is the line between nodes. Such nodes form the track's movement trajectory.
Senoz: The nodes on the left and right represent the positions of the track's left and right ends. This type of track is called [b]L/R[/b] track.

Senoz: [b]Left Click[/b] any node or line to select it and you will enter node editing mode, which switches the Note preview to node preview. At this point [b]Right Click[/b] to create a node at the corresponding position.
Senoz: Give it a try! Select a track, then create 3 nodes on each side and see how the track movement changes.
Senoz: Initially created nodes won't make the track move smoothly. When the time reaches the next node it will immediately jump to that node's position.
Senoz: To make the track move smoothly, just select a node and press [b]Tab[/b]. Pressing Tab multiple times cycles through different easing modes.

Senoz: The default easing curves are all sine function curves. To switch to different curve families, press [b]- Minus key[/b] to pop up the easing selection panel, or press number keys to quickly switch.
Senoz: Give it a try! Switch the easing mode 5 times, and switch curve family once.
Senoz: Now, you've learned all the basic elements of making a TAKUMI³ custom chart. The introductory tutorial ends here.
Senoz: You can continue familiarizing yourself with other features in this practice area, or create a real new project.
Senoz: If you want to see the documentation, please click the 'View Tutorial' button in the tools panel, and then you can jump to the website in there.
Senoz: More interactive tutorials are also in that panel, and you can experience them more than once.
Senoz: Finally, looking forward to the amazing charts you'll create. See you then!
Let's start with chart projects. The folder that holds all the files needed for chart editing is called the chart project folder.

A chart project contains quite a few files: the music file, cover art file, a song info file that records the song name, difficulty and such, and a preference file that stores the scroll speed, BPM list and the like.
The chart files for each difficulty are also there — Normal, Hard, Master, Insanity, and Ravage — five difficulties in total.
The editor knows how to read these files through the t3proj file in the folder. If you need to change which files it reads, you can open that file with Notepad — it has detailed comments inside.
By default, saved chart files have the [b]editing.json[/b] extension, like [b]master.editing.json[/b]. This is because editing inevitably introduces content that only the editor needs.
When you package the chart project into a playable t3pkg file in the editor, this editor-specific content is automatically cleaned up, and the chart file extension is changed to [b].json[/b].
As you continue working, the chart project folder will gain two subfolders: AutoSave and Logs, which store autosaved files and error logs respectively.
If something goes wrong during editing, please submit the log file from Logs for the current date to the author via an issue or other means.
Now for some things worth noting. First, all time values in the editor are integers in milliseconds.
The center of the preview has an x-coordinate of 0, the preview boundaries are at ±5, and the in-game play boundaries at ±4.5.
When editing a chart, it's easy to accidentally introduce hard-to-find errors, like placing a Note on a track with zero width.
When that happens, click the small arrow at the bottom of the interface and select the Format Chart function from the toolbar — it will automatically fix potential errors in your chart.
Before packaging into a playable file, be sure to run the formatter at least once.
